title: Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Net](#dod:net-oda) [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
caption:
Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita
Net official development assistance (ODA), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and foreign aid contributions, plotting GDP per capita on the horizontal axis against net official development assistance given per capita on the vertical axis. The horizontal axis measures GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, ranging from zero to $120,000, while the vertical axis measures aid given in constant 2023 US dollars, ranging from zero to over $800. Circles represent individual countries, sized by population and colored by region, with Europe shown in blue, North America in red, Asia in teal, and Oceania in light blue. A positive relationship is visible across the chart, where countries with higher GDP per capita generally appear higher on the vertical axis, indicating larger per capita aid contributions. Norway occupies the highest position on the vertical axis, exceeding $800 in aid per capita, followed by Luxembourg and Switzerland in the $400 to $500 range. Ireland and Sweden also appear in the upper section of the chart with values above $400. The United States is positioned near the $200 mark on the vertical axis but sits further to the right on the horizontal axis with a GDP per capita around $70,000, distinguished by a large circle size indicating a large population. Qatar appears on the far right with a GDP per capita exceeding $110,000 and aid contributions around $250. In the lower left quadrant, countries such as Thailand, Kazakhstan, and Turkey show lower GDP per capita figures between $20,000 and $40,000, with aid contributions remaining below $100 per capita. Japan and South Korea appear in the middle of the distribution with GDP per capita around $40,000 to $50,000 and aid contributions between $100 and $200. The data shows a general trend where wealthier nations contribute more foreign aid per person, though the specific amounts vary significantly among high-income countries.
